title: Selection

publication.changeSummary: Topic-specific draft for Selection; requires human source, technical, pedagogy, and accessibility review. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: CS2023 Software Development Fundamentals; August 2023; section applicable to Selection
sources.claim: Selection evaluates Boolean conditions to choose the one appropriate path, with branch order and boundary comparisons determining which path handles each input.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Selection evaluates Boolean conditions to choose the one appropriate path, with branch order and boundary comparisons determining which path handles each input.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A grading rule tests score at least 90 before score at least 75 so a score of 95 reaches the highest band instead of the first broad match. Your goal is to design and trace nested and multi-branch decisions including boundary cases. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Expressions and evaluation; Sequence. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Operational model
sections.body: Selection evaluates Boolean conditions to choose the one appropriate path, with branch order and boundary comparisons determining which path handles each input. The learner should use this model to predict an observable result rather than merely repeat terminology.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure point
sections.body: Overlapping conditions, unreachable branches, missing default behavior, and floating-point comparisons can make an apparently complete decision incomplete or order-dependent. A correct explanation therefore includes the applicable assumptions and avoids this common mistake: Separate if statements and one if-else-if chain always have identical behavior when several conditions are true.

includes: Use Boolean conditions to choose among mutually appropriate paths.
Design and trace nested and multi-branch decisions including boundary cases.
excludes: Overlapping conditions, unreachable branches, missing default behavior, and floating-point comparisons can make an apparently complete decision incomplete or order-dependent.

outcomes: Design and trace nested and multi-branch decisions including boundary cases.

examples.title: Selection: direct case
examples.situation: A grading rule tests score at least 90 before score at least 75 so a score of 95 reaches the highest band instead of the first broad match.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the starting evidence: A grading rule tests score at least 90 before score at least 75 so a score of 95 reaches the highest band instead of the first broad match.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.

Step 3 — Check the result against this correction: Choose independent or mutually exclusive branches deliberately, order overlapping conditions from specific to broad, and test every boundary and default path.
examples.result: The case supports the declared outcome because Choose independent or mutually exclusive branches deliberately, order overlapping conditions from specific to broad, and test every boundary and default path.

examples.title: Selection: boundary case
examples.situation: A delivery rule handles negative distance as invalid, zero as pickup, up to five as local, and larger values as extended delivery.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed context: A delivery rule handles negative distance as invalid, zero as pickup, up to five as local, and larger values as extended delivery.

Step 2 — Keep this boundary explicit: Overlapping conditions, unreachable branches, missing default behavior, and floating-point comparisons can make an apparently complete decision incomplete or order-dependent.
Step 3 — Reject the documented misconception: Separate if statements and one if-else-if chain always have identical behavior when several conditions are true.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Design and trace nested and multi-branch decisions including boundary cases.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Separate if statements and one if-else-if chain always have identical behavior when several conditions are true.
misconceptions.correction: Choose independent or mutually exclusive branches deliberately, order overlapping conditions from specific to broad, and test every boundary and default path.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using the direct and boundary scenarios, explain why this belief fails: Separate if statements and one if-else-if chain always have identical behavior when several conditions are true.

analogy.example: A route with ordered checkpoints, forks, and repeated laps can model Selection while each decision uses the current conditions.
usefulFor: Visualizing order, branching, repetition, progress, and nested routes
limitations: Real control flow can include calls, exceptions, concurrency, and evaluation rules absent from a physical route.
Overlapping conditions, unreachable branches, missing default behavior, and floating-point comparisons can make an apparently complete decision incomplete or order-dependent.

Line 3: give the result and reject the common mistake.

recap.summary: You should now be able to design and trace nested and multi-branch decisions including boundary cases. Keep this boundary in view: Overlapping conditions, unreachable branches, missing default behavior, and floating-point comparisons can make an apparently complete decision incomplete or order-dependent.
retrievalPrompts: Explain Selection without using the optional analogy and include one observable consequence.
Why is this statement incorrect: Separate if statements and one if-else-if chain always have identical behavior when several conditions are true.
Apply the model to this case from memory: A delivery rule handles negative distance as invalid, zero as pickup, up to five as local, and larger values as extended delivery.
recap.nextReviewPrompt: Next: continue to “Iteration”. Before moving on, answer one retrieval prompt without reopening the explanation.
